data.title: Large-Refactor Review Path
data.description:
## Context
The current code review workflow handles feature additions and targeted bug fixes well, but breaks down for large cross-cutting refactors — commits touching 20 or more files with a uniform transformation applied across them (renames, signature changes, import path migrations). Two failure modes occur today: the 600-line limit rejects large diffs outright with no usable alternative, and the classifier misscores uniform mechanical changes as low-complexity, routing them to haiku reviewers that lack the context to cover the full diff. Engineers get either a blocked review or unreliable findings on their largest structural changes.

## Success Criteria
- A commit touching >=20 files (excluding .test-index) is automatically diverted from REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md into a new REVIEW-WORKFLOW-HUGE.md without any manual intervention
- REVIEW-WORKFLOW-HUGE identifies confirmed refactors by sampling 7 stratified files and requiring >=5/7 to show a consistent transformation pattern; confirmed refactors proceed through haiku batch agents + a new refactor-anomaly opus reviewer that checks only flagged anomalous files
- Non-refactor large commits (pattern confidence below threshold) return to the standard review path; before dispatching any sub-agent the workflow emits a MODEL_OVERRIDE: opus signal, and all tier sub-agents are instantiated with the opus model regardless of classifier tier; the final findings file is identical in schema to the standard review path output
- When a diff exceeds 600 lines the classifier appends SIZE_WARNING: <line_count> lines to its output and continues processing; no diff is ever returned with REJECTED status solely due to line count; verifiable by staging a 700-line commit and asserting the workflow produces a findings file with no rejection status
- The refactor-anomaly opus reviewer emits a structured findings block with one entry per anomalous file, each carrying a dimension label (pattern_conformance | behavioral_drift | callsite_completeness), a severity (critical | important | minor), and a description; the review is considered passing when no critical findings are present
- .test-index is excluded from the file count threshold check
- The diversion threshold is configurable via review.huge_diff_file_threshold (default: 20, must be a positive integer) in dso-config.conf; setting 0 or a negative integer is rejected with a validation error at threshold-check time
- The classifier-size-output.md contract is updated to replace size_action: reject with size_action: warn; all consumers branching on size_action are verified to handle the new value before the change ships
- CONFIGURATION-REFERENCE.md and dso-config.example.conf include the new review.huge_diff_file_threshold key with default value (20) and valid range (positive integers only)
- If fewer than 7 files are eligible for sampling after .test-index and binary/generated file exclusions, sampling is skipped and the commit is treated as low confidence, routing directly to the fallback path

## Scenario Analysis
11 scenarios survived red/blue team review:
- Haiku batch agent cap overflow (critical, runtime): underdefined grouping strategy can produce more batches than orchestration.max_agents allows, causing silent coverage drop
- Anomaly reviewer timeout on large flagged-file sets (critical, runtime): opus reviewer receiving many anomalous files may time out, producing zero findings on the files most likely to contain bugs
- Schema incompatibility on deploy (critical, deployment): refactor-anomaly agent output dimensions must be validated against the review gate schema before shipping
- SIZE_WARNING downstream consumers (high, runtime): consumers expecting hard rejection will silently pass through; SC8 requires consumer audit before ship
- Multi-pattern false confidence (high, runtime): commits mixing two distinct transform types may pass 5/7 consensus on the dominant pattern while minority-cluster files escape review
- REVIEW-WORKFLOW-HUGE.md absent on first deploy (high, deployment): diversion check must fail gracefully if target file is missing
- Agent-routing.conf not updated on upgrade (high, deployment): refactor-anomaly agent registration must ship atomically with the agent file
- .test-index path format mismatch (medium, configuration): exclusion logic must normalize paths before comparison

## Dependencies
None

## Approach
Add a file-count threshold check early in REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md that diverts large commits (>=20 changed files, excluding .test-index) to a new REVIEW-WORKFLOW-HUGE.md. That workflow runs a cheap haiku pattern extraction step (7 stratified files, consensus-based — >=5/7 must agree for high confidence) to classify the commit as a confirmed refactor or not, then routes accordingly: confirmed refactor path uses haiku batch agents plus a new refactor-anomaly opus reviewer for anomalous files; fallback path returns to the standard classifier with an opus model override for all sub-agents. The 600-line hard rejection in the classifier is replaced with a non-blocking SIZE_WARNING signal, and the classifier-size-output.md contract is updated accordingly.
